articles.title: PostgreSQL and the OOM Killer: Why We Use Strict Memory Overcommit
articles.summary: PostgreSQL's use of strict memory overcommit helps prevent out-of-memory errors by limiting the amount of RAM allocated to the database. This approach ensures that even if the system runs low on memory, PostgreSQL can continue to function without crashing. By default, PostgreSQL uses a technique called "oom killer" which terminates processes when the available memory falls below a certain threshold. However, this can lead to performance issues and crashes. Strict memory overcommit allows PostgreSQL to allocate more memory than the available RAM, thus reducing the likelihood of an oom kill. This approach is particularly important for large-scale applications that require high-performance storage.

Screwworms were once a major problem in the US, but a decades-long program to eradicate them has successfully eliminated the parasite. The US was free of screwworms due to this program, which used sterile male screwworm flies to breed out the native males and eventually eliminate the pest. Now, efforts are underway to restore the barrier at Panama, but it will likely take years for screwworms to be eradicated from North and Central America once again.

articles.title: Diving into the depths of Widevine L3
articles.summary: Widevine is a DRM scheme used to securely deliver content to end users. It requires three servers: Provisioning Server, License Server, and Content Server. The root of trust is the keybox, which contains device information and cryptographic keys. Widevine has three security levels, including L1, L2, and L3, each with different requirements for handling DRM keys and decrypted content. Breaking Widevine has been attempted in recent years due to its vulnerabilities, but Frida hooks have been used to dump device keys from running sessions without requiring root access.

articles.title: Until_Heat_Death_Do_Us_Part: Counting until the heat death of the universe, broadcasting counter every second over 100Mbps ethernet
articles.summary: Counting until the heat death of the universe, broadcasting counter every second over 100Mbps ethernet, is a project that involves creating an ASIC with a large enough counter to theoretically count until the heat death of the universe, and then broadcasting an ethernet frame over 100Mbps ethernet with the current counters value. This is achieved by incrementing the counter every 20ns and broadcasting every second, assuming the universe dies in approximately $10^{100}$ years. The project also includes software support for sniffing local network traffic and parsing ethernet frames, including a packet_receiver program that can be used to build and run the counter.
